summary: Scorching Garcia guitar, tight jams & stellar Terrapin Station > Playing In The Band flow
blurb: The May 6‑1978 Vermont show bursts with fire on Sugaree, Peggy‑O and a blistering first set. A seamless second set delivers Terrapin Station into Playing In The Band, a thunderous Drums/Space/Wharf Rat stretch and a joyous Sugar Magnolia encore. Multiple recordings capture the energy, making this a must‑listen for spring‑78 fans.
review: On May 6, 1978 at Patrick Field House the Dead were in top form. Jerry Garcia’s tone was described as “on fire” from Sugaree through Big River and the climactic Deal jam, with soaring leads that many reviewers called perfect, shimmering and scorching. Phil Lesh finally got his bass turned up loud enough to be heard, laying down a solid foundation and even dropping a memorable descending three‑note phrase on Me & My Uncle; his counterpoint work in the Grand Unction outro earned particular praise. Bob Weir provided tasteful slide work on Sugaree and held his own vocal duties while Donna Jean’s soaring backing added extra color. The second set was a masterclass in Dead improvisation: Terrapin Station flowed into Playing In The Band, leading to an intense Drums > Space > Wharf Rat sequence that reviewers called “thunderous” and a “galaxy of greatness.” The show closed on a high‑energy Sugar Magnolia and a raucous One More Saturday Night encore. Across the board, recordings range from crystal‑clear audience tapes to an excellent matrix capture; the latter (gd78-05-06.sbd-aud.cotsman.13726) offers warm soundboard clarity with just enough audience ambience to feel truly live, making it the best listening source.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Strong positive sentiment across five recordings (Terrapin, Playing In The Band, Sugaree, Peggy-O, Deal) with consistent praise for Jerry’s guitar, Phil’s bass and the overall set flow; only two negatives and two mixed reviews, so a high positive adjustment is warranted.

best_recording.reason: Excellent matrix capture blends warm soundboard clarity with live audience ambience; highlights Jerry’s fire, solid bass and the intense Drums > Wharf Rat segment.

key_highlights: Jerry Garcia’s scorching guitar tone throughout, especially on Sugaree and Deal.
First‑set energy with blazing performances of Peggy‑O, Looks Like Rain and Deal.
Second‑set masterclass: Terrapin Station → Playing In The Band → Drums/Space → Wharf Rat segue.
Excellent matrix recording provides warm SBD sound plus intimate audience presence.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s tone was repeatedly called perfect, scorching and on fire; his leads were soaring, his vocals strong, and he delivered a full‑force performance after recent laryngitis concerns.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh’s bass rose to the forefront, with listeners asking for it turned up louder; solid support throughout, highlighted by a descending three‑note phrase on Me & My Uncle and “crazy counterpoint slices” in the Grand Unction outro.
band_performance.Bob: Weir contributed tasteful slide work on Sugaree and maintained solid rhythm guitar; his vocals helped cover weaker backing from Donna Jean, adding to the overall vocal chemistry.
band_performance.Keith: Keyboard presence was spotty—often buried or invisible—but shone briefly on New Minglewood Blues, Peggy‑O and an outro into drums, offering a pretty touch when it emerged.
band_performance.Mickey: Delivered thunderous, pre‑Beast and Beam style rhythms; part of the intense Drums > Wharf Rat showcase that was praised as a highlight.
band_performance.Billy: Provided driving energy alongside Mickey, contributing to the “galaxy of greatness” in the Deal jam and the powerful second‑set percussion work.
